card.intuition: A new architectural pattern where components can be rendered entirely on the server and sent to the browser as a specialized stream, reducing the amount of JavaScript sent to the client.
card.analogy: Ordering a pre-built Lego set: instead of receiving the instructions and all the pieces (Client Components), you receive the already-assembled 'Dragon' (Server Component). It takes up no space in your backpack (no JS bundle), but you can't change its pose (no interactivity) unless it has some 'interative joints' (Client Islands).
when_to_use: Data-heavy pages where you want minimal JS on the client (like a Blog or E-commerce list)
Securely accessing databases or secret API keys directly from a component
Reducing the 'Waterfall' of network requests in the browser
gotchas: RSC CANNOT use hooks like `useState` or `useEffect` because they don't exist on the server
They are NOT the same as Server-Side Rendering (SSR); SSR produces HTML, RSC produces a specialized binary format that React uses to update the UI

exercise.prompt: Identify the directive used at the top of a file to mark a component as interactive (client-side).
hints: Where does it run? On the ______
exercise.test_code:
def check(ans): return ans.lower() == "client"
assert check("client")
print("PASS")
